aksAutomaticSummary.message: Fix blocker findings before migrating workloads to a new AKS Automatic cluster.

AKSBP001.Name: Allowed Container Images Policy Enforcement
AKSBP001.Recommendation: Deploy the Azure Policy initiative 'Kubernetes cluster pod security restricted standards' and configure specific allowed container registries. Use 'az policy assignment create' to assign the policy and set enforcement to 'deny' mode for production environments.

Items.Issue: Deploy the Azure Policy initiative 'Kubernetes cluster pod security restricted standards' and configure specific allowed container registries. Use 'az policy assignment create' to assign the policy and set enforcement to 'deny' mode for production environments.

AKSBP001.FailMessage: Container image restriction policies are not enforced, allowing deployment of images from any registry including public registries, untrusted sources, or images with known vulnerabilities. This significantly increases supply chain attack risks and compliance violations.

AKSBP002.Name: No Privileged Containers Policy Enforcement
AKSBP002.Recommendation: Enable the 'Do not allow privileged containers' Azure Policy definition in enforce mode. Use Pod Security Standards with 'restricted' profile to block privileged containers and ensure security baseline compliance.

Items.Issue: Enable the 'Do not allow privileged containers' Azure Policy definition in enforce mode. Use Pod Security Standards with 'restricted' profile to block privileged containers and ensure security baseline compliance.

AKSBP002.FailMessage: Privileged container policies are not enforced, allowing workloads to run with full root privileges, access host devices, mount host file systems, and potentially escape container boundaries. This creates severe security risks and violates least-privilege principles.

AKSBP003.Name: Multiple Node Pools
AKSBP003.Recommendation: Create separate node pools for different workload types using 'az aks nodepool add --resource-group <rg> --cluster-name <cluster> --name <pool-name>'. Use system pools for system pods, user pools for applications, and specialized pools (GPU, memory-optimized) for specific workloads.

Items.Issue: Create separate node pools for different workload types using 'az aks nodepool add --resource-group <rg> --cluster-name <cluster> --name <pool-name>'. Use system pools for system pods, user pools for applications, and specialized pools (GPU, memory-optimized) for specific workloads.

AKSBP003.FailMessage: Single node pool configuration limits workload isolation, scaling flexibility, and security boundaries. All workloads share the same VM size, OS configuration, and scaling parameters, making it impossible to optimize for different application requirements or implement proper security zones.

🛠️ Clean Up Orphaned ConfigMaps

  • Verify: Check usage (kubectl describe cm ).
  • Delete: kubectl delete cm if unused.
  • Automation: Schedule periodic scans.

NET001.Name: Services Without Endpoints
NET001.Description: Identifies services that have no backing endpoints.

Recommendation.text: Check if the service selector matches any pods. Ensure the backing pods are running and ready.
Recommendation.html:
🔍 Services Without Endpoints

  • Verify that your service has a valid selector.
  • Check if pods exist and are ready in the same namespace.
  • Use kubectl describe svc <name> and kubectl get endpointslices -n <namespace> -l kubernetes.io/service-name=<name>.
  • Restart affected pods or fix labels as needed.

⚠️ Often caused by label mismatches or no pods running.

  • Use internal IP ranges or private LoadBalancers where possible.
  • Restrict NodePort usage or protect with firewall rules.

  • Consider network policies or service mesh for access control.

Recommendation.text: Fix invalid ingress definitions including missing TLS secrets, backend services, and path issues.

🌐 Ingress Health Remediation

  • Add spec.ingressClassName or annotations if missing.
  • Validate all backend services and ports exist.
  • Fix missing TLS secrets or use valid ones.
  • Avoid duplicate host/path combinations.
  • Use only valid pathTypes: Exact, Prefix, or ImplementationSpecific.

📌 This includes:

- backend services or ports not found

NET005.Name: Ingress Host/Path Conflicts
NET005.Description: Detects duplicate host/path combinations across ingresses in the same namespace.

Recommendation.text: Resolve conflicting host and path combinations across Ingress resources to ensure predictable routing. Each host/path pair should be unique.
Recommendation.html:
🚫 Resolve Ingress Conflicts

  • Ensure that each unique host and path combination is defined in only one Ingress resource.
  • Use specific hostnames instead of broad wildcards where possible to prevent unintended conflicts.
  • Review your Ingress definitions for overlapping rules and consolidate or adjust as necessary.
  • Test routing after making changes to confirm correct behavior.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Checking for Ingress resources with overlapping host/path rules.
📌 Conflicts can lead to unexpected routing of traffic.
⚠️ Ensure each host/path combination is unique across ingresses.

NET006.Description: Detects ingress rules that use wildcard hosts.

Recommendation.text: Review Ingress resources using wildcard hosts. Prefer explicit hostnames for tighter security and clearer routing intent where possible.
Recommendation.html:
⭐ Review Wildcard Ingresses

  • Evaluate if a wildcard host is truly necessary for the application's routing requirements.
  • Where possible, replace wildcards with specific hostnames to limit unintentional exposure.
  • Ensure that security policies and firewalls are in place to control access to wildcard-enabled Ingresses.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Checking for Ingresses with wildcard hostnames.

⚠️ Consider using explicit hostnames for tighter security.

NET007.Name: Service TargetPort Mismatch
NET007.Description: Detects services whose targetPort does not exist on backing pods.

Recommendation.text: Ensure that all 'targetPort' definitions in services correctly match a 'containerPort' (by number or name) defined in the pods selected by the service.
Recommendation.html:
🎯 Fix Service TargetPort Mismatches

  • Verify the targetPort in your Service definition. It should either be a numerical port or a named port.
  • Check the containerPorts in the Pods selected by the Service.
  • Ensure the `targetPort` (by number) or `name` (for named ports) in the Pod's `containerPort` matches the Service's `targetPort`.
  • A common fix is to ensure consistent naming conventions or directly use port numbers.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Checking if service 'targetPort' matches a pod's 'containerPort'.
📌 A mismatch means the service can't route traffic to the pods.
⚠️ This is a common cause of service connectivity issues.

NET007.Message: No issues detected for Service TargetPort Mismatch.

NET008.Name: ExternalName Service to Internal IP
NET008.Description: Identifies ExternalName services that point to internal IP addresses.

Recommendation.text: Review 'ExternalName' services pointing to internal IPs. Consider using a regular Service with an EndpointSlice for internal traffic, or clarify intent if valid.
Recommendation.html:
🔄 Review ExternalName to Internal IP

  • ExternalName services are primarily for CNAME-like redirection to external DNS names.
  • If routing to an internal IP address, consider if a standard `Service` with manually created `EndpointSlice` or a `Service` with `type: ClusterIP` backed by pods is more appropriate.
  • Ensure this configuration is intentional and does not bypass intended network segmentation or security policies.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Checking ExternalName services for internal IP targets.
📌 ExternalName services are for external DNS.
⚠️ Pointing to internal IPs can be unusual or a misconfig.

NET008.Message: No issues detected for ExternalName Service to Internal IP.

NET009.Name: Overly Permissive Network Policy
NET009.Description: Identifies NetworkPolicies with empty rules or broad all-IP blocks.

Recommendation.text: Review NetworkPolicies with empty rules or broad IP blocks. Ensure policies enforce the principle of least privilege, explicitly defining allowed traffic.
Recommendation.html:
🔐 Restrict Overly Permissive Network Policies

  • Ensure `policyTypes` are paired with explicit `ingress` and `egress` rules that define allowed traffic.
  • Avoid empty `ingress` or `egress` sections if the `policyTypes` are defined, as this defaults to allowing all traffic for that type.
  • Limit the use of `ipBlock: 0.0.0.0/0`. Instead, define specific CIDR ranges for necessary external communication.
  • Adopt a "deny-by-default" approach and explicitly allow only required communication.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Checking NetworkPolicies for overly permissive rules.
📌 Empty rules or '0.0.0.0/0' can open up your network.
⚠️ Ensure policies only allow explicitly needed traffic.

NET009.Message: No issues detected for Overly Permissive Network Policy.

NET010.Name: Network Policy Overly Permissive IPBlock
NET010.Description: Flags NetworkPolicies that allow 0.0.0.0/0 through ipBlock rules.

Recommendation.text: Replace '0.0.0.0/0' ipBlock in NetworkPolicies with specific CIDR ranges to enforce the principle of least privilege. Only allow traffic to/from necessary IPs.
Recommendation.html:
🚫 Restrict '0.0.0.0/0' in Network Policies

  • Avoid using `ipBlock: 0.0.0.0/0` in NetworkPolicies unless absolutely required for specific, well-understood use cases (e.g., public internet access).
  • Identify the precise CIDR ranges or specific IP addresses that need to be allowed.
  • For egress, if public internet access is needed, consider egress gateways or more restrictive network policies to control outbound traffic.
  • This is a critical security vulnerability if unintended.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Looking for NetworkPolicies that allow all IPs.

Recommendation.html:
• If GitOps/Helm controls requests, consider VPA updateMode: Off or Initial.
  • Avoid overlapping HPA (CPU/memory) and VPA ownership without clear boundaries.
  • Document which controller owns requests per workload.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Some VPA configurations may fight with declarative config or HPA.
⚠️ Clarify autoscaling ownership boundaries.

WRK011.Message: No issues detected for VPA Update Mode and Declarative Resource Conflict Risk.

WRK012.Name: PodDisruptionBudget Adequacy for Replicated Workloads
WRK012.Description: Validates that replicated workloads have matching PDBs with sensible settings.

Recommendation.text: Add or tune PDBs so maintenance can proceed safely without over-restricting disruption.
Recommendation.html:
• Ensure replicated workloads (2+ replicas) have a matching PDB.
  • Avoid minAvailable equal to replica count for normal maintenance windows.
  • Use pragmatic budgets (for example maxUnavailable: 1 for many workloads).
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Checking if PDB coverage and settings are practical.
⚠️ Missing or overly strict PDBs can block maintenance and upgrades.

WRK012.Message: No issues detected for PodDisruptionBudget Adequacy for Replicated Workloads.

WRK013.Name: CrashLoopBackOff and OOMKilled Guardrail
WRK013.Description: Flags pods with CrashLoopBackOff, OOMKilled state, or high restart counts.

Recommendation.text: Stabilize CrashLoop/OOM pods before applying aggressive right-sizing changes.
Recommendation.html:
• Investigate container logs and termination reasons for recurring restarts.
  • Increase memory requests/limits when OOMKilled events are observed.
  • Apply sizing changes gradually and validate SLO/error rates.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Found CrashLoopBackOff or OOMKilled signals.
⚠️ Treat right-sizing for these pods with extra caution.

WRK013.Message: No issues detected for CrashLoopBackOff and OOMKilled Guardrail.

WRK014.Name: Missing Memory Limits
WRK014.Description: Checks that every container has an explicit memory limit.

Recommendation.text: Define a memory limit on all containers to reduce the risk of node OOM pressure.
Recommendation.html:
• Add resources.limits.memory to every application and init container.
  • Set the limit high enough for normal peaks, then tune requests separately.
  • Review workloads with kubectl get deploy,statefulset,daemonset -A -o yaml to confirm the source manifests carry the limit.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Some containers are missing memory limits.
📌 That can increase the blast radius of memory spikes on shared nodes.
⚠️ Set explicit memory limits at the source manifest.

WRK014.Message: No issues detected for Missing Memory Limits.

WRK015.Name: Replicated Workloads Missing Spread Constraints
WRK015.Description: Detects replicated workloads that define neither anti-affinity nor topology spread constraints.

Recommendation.text: Add pod anti-affinity or topology spread constraints to replicated workloads.
Recommendation.html:
• Add topologySpreadConstraints or affinity.podAntiAffinity to each workload with multiple replicas.
  • Prefer distribution across nodes and zones using stable labels such as topology.kubernetes.io/zone and kubernetes.io/hostname.
  • Update the source Deployment, StatefulSet, or Helm values so the spreading rule is maintained on future releases.
SpeechBubble: 🤖 Some replicated workloads do not declare any pod spreading rules.
📌 AKS Automatic can reject those workloads to avoid single-node placement.
⚠️ Add topology spread or pod anti-affinity at the source manifest.

WRK015.AutomaticMutationOutcome: Observed on AKS Automatic: replicated workloads without pod anti-affinity or topology spread constraints can be denied at admission.
WRK015.Message: No issues detected for Replicated Workloads Missing Spread Constraints.

summary.message: Fix blocker findings before migrating workloads to a new AKS Automatic cluster.

blockers.name: Container images do not use latest tag
blockers.failMessage: Container order-service: Image uses latest tag
blockers.recommendation: Specify an explicit image tag (e.g., ':v1.2.3') on every container and initContainer to ensure consistent deployments.
blockers.admissionNote: AKS Automatic may deny this resource in enforce mode. AKS Deployment Safeguards can deny workloads that use the latest tag or omit an explicit version tag.

warnings.name: Pods Running as Root
warnings.failMessage: Container order-service runs as root or has no runAsUser set
warnings.recommendation: Avoid running pods as root by explicitly setting runAsUser to a non-zero UID in pod or container securityContext.

warnings.name: Seccomp Profile Not Configured
warnings.failMessage: Container order-service has no explicit seccomp profile
warnings.recommendation: Set seccompProfile.type to RuntimeDefault or Localhost at the pod or container level.
warnings.admissionNote: AKS Automatic surfaces this as a warning and does not auto-mutate it.

warnings.name: Missing Readiness and Liveness Probes
warnings.recommendation: Add readiness and liveness probes to all containers to improve availability and fault detection.

items.name: Azure Linux as Host OS
items.recommendation: Migrate to Azure Linux by creating new node pools with 'az aks nodepool add --os-sku AzureLinux', then migrate workloads and delete old pools. Note: In-place OS SKU changes are not supported, requiring node pool replacement.

items.name: Deployment Safeguards Enabled
items.failMessage: Deployment Safeguards are disabled, allowing non-compliant workloads to be deployed without validation of Kubernetes best practices. This leads to deployments without resource requests/limits, missing health probes, no anti-affinity rules, and other configuration issues that impact reliability and cost.
items.recommendation: Enable Deployment Safeguards using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --safeguards-level Warning' for alerting or 'Enforcement' to block non-compliant deployments. This enforces best practices including resource requests, readiness/liveness probes, pod anti-affinity, and Pod Security Standards.

items.name: Control Plane SLA
items.recommendation: Upgrade to Standard tier using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --tier Standard' to get 99.95% uptime SLA, financially-backed availability guarantees, and improved support. This is essential for production workloads requiring high availability.

items.name: Workload Identity Enabled
items.recommendation: Enable Workload Identity using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --enable-workload-identity' (requires OIDC issuer). Create Kubernetes service accounts and federate them with Azure managed identities for secure, token-based authentication to Azure services.

items.name: Azure CNI with Cilium Dataplane Recommended
items.recommendation: For new clusters, use '--network-plugin azure --network-dataplane cilium --network-plugin-mode overlay' for optimal performance. Azure CNI powered by Cilium provides eBPF-based packet processing, better scalability, and advanced L3-L7 network policies. Existing clusters should migrate by creating a new cluster with Cilium enabled.

items.name: Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) is enabled
items.recommendation: Enable VPA using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --enable-vpa'. Deploy VPA objects with 'updateMode: Auto' or 'Off' for recommendations only. Monitor VPA recommendations and adjust application resource requests/limits accordingly for better resource efficiency.

items.name: KEDA (Event-Driven Autoscaling) Enabled
items.recommendation: Enable KEDA using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --enable-keda'. Deploy ScaledObject resources to define event sources (Azure Queue, Service Bus, Kafka, HTTP, etc.) and scaling behavior. KEDA complements HPA by enabling scale-to-zero and event-driven scaling patterns.

items.name: Node Auto-provisioning or Cluster Autoscaler Configured
items.recommendation: Enable Node Auto-provisioning using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --node-provisioning-mode Auto' for Karpenter-based dynamic provisioning. Alternatively, enable Cluster Autoscaler with 'az aks update --enable-cluster-autoscaler'. NAP is recommended for modern workloads with diverse resource requirements.

items.name: OIDC Issuer Enabled
items.recommendation: Enable OIDC issuer using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --enable-oidc-issuer'. This enables workload identity federation, allowing pods to authenticate to Azure services using service account tokens instead of secrets.

items.name: Image Cleaner Enabled
items.failMessage: Image Cleaner is disabled, allowing stale and potentially vulnerable container images to accumulate on node disks. This increases storage costs, extends attack surface with outdated images containing known CVEs, and can impact node performance due to disk space consumption.
items.recommendation: Enable Image Cleaner using 'az aks update --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --enable-image-cleaner'. Configure cleaning interval and retention policies to automatically remove unused container images and reduce attack surface.
